text: The time limit doesn't necessarily make it harder, it just changes how you approach the games and puzzles. The time limit is more of a mechanic about making choices for a given cycle rather than a difficulty stressor. It's a really unique mechanic that makes this game stand apart, and it's worth trying through the first dungeon to get a feel for if you want to keep going. It also tells some beautiful and poignant stories, and you really get attached to that world. No game spoilers, but some mechanics descriptions that i spoiler tagged in case you wanted a truly blind experience and only wanted a 'yes-no' above. The game is built around a 3 day cycle where you choose what you want ti accomplish. It's a lot more time than it sounds like, and there are ways to further manipulate time. And it's not a true 'reset'. Certain items, consumable resource items like arrows, bombs, rupees reset, but progression and major items are saved. (There's also a mechanism to accumulate rupees between cycles.) Things like dungeon puzzles will reset, but the items you get will act as shortcuts so if you do run out of time you aren't starting from scratch, and you can rematch bosses. Certain character events have multiple outcomes where you can make different things happen on different cycles to collect everything
label: Argument
notes: The 'Because' Test: Provides an extensive breakdown of the 3-day cycle, items, and progression mechanics.

text: It's a great game, but you should go into it with the right headspace: There is a time limit, but there are also checkpoints in the form of progress you can take with you to future loops, as well as ocarina songs that slow down time, so it's pretty forgiving. Still, it's fun to go in with a speedrunner's mindset at times, especially if you're trying to collect the 'stray faeries' in a dungeon, which must be done in a single loop. The vibe is a little dark. Embrace the gloomy atmosphere. Some side quests require you to be at the right place at the right time, and it's not always intuitive. Some things you just stumble upon. So if you're really stuck on something, don't feel bad about checking a guide. Like for example, I often got tripped up with the mailman-related side quest, not realizing what I'm actually supposed to do is drop in on the mayor. If you like time loop games, also check out Outer Wilds.
label: Argument
notes: The 'Because' Test: Cites ocarina songs, stray faeries, and the mailman quest to support the advice.

Multiple players assumed the broken Master Sword would've been used as an always-acessible Fuse handle, which would line up with how Fujibayashi tends to write his games, but it gets thrown away for Zelda. Nothing about the zonai past correlates logically with the present day events. It's mostly just a story used to justify the skydiving finale and shoving the characters along to make it work. The game could've been fairly satisfying even as a standalone title without making references to past ones if it was actually coherent with itself.
label: Argument

text: I feel like I'm the only person on this sub who understands Nintendo makes family games for a family audiences, not epics for hardcore gamers who want to pick apart every part of the lore like it's some religious text. Of course you're going to be disappointed that there isn't some overarching thread connecting the games, because that was never an explicit intention to begin with. From the Zelda timeline zelda wiki entry: 'Famitsu asked series producer Eiji Aonuma, and Breath of the Wild director Hidemaro Fujibayashi what the changes meant, and this was their reply: Eiji Aonuma, series producer: 'Well of course it's at the very end. But, I get what you're asking, it's which timeline is it the end of?' Hidemaro Fujibayashi, director: 'That's... up to the player's imagination, isn't it?' Aonuma: 'Hyrule's history changes with time. When we think of the next game and what we want to do with it, we might think, 'Oh, this'll fit well', and place it neatly into the timeline, but sometimes we think, 'Oh crap', and have to change the placement. Actually, the decided history has been tweaked many times.' (laughs)' - The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild Has Been Added To The Series' Official Timeline, Siliconera. It's clear that the 'official zelda timeline' is just some fun, hypothetical thoughtwork from Nintendo to appease fans and not something that's written in stone that all games will follow or abide by.
label: Argument

text: BOTW had fans theorizing, the devs noticed and acknowledged that and said in an interview that they don't want to give the placement away because they like how people come up with their own things. But they also said that there is a placement, that you should be able to figure out where it is looking in-game, and that it's at the end of a single timeline. Creating a Champion says Link won in OOT, so it's in the adult timeline. TOTK interviews say even more, that they 'don't do things randomly' and that 'the lore isn't meant to be broken down'. Then an EOW interview says that they saw backlash at TOTK'S story and had a 'story bootcamp' for EOW. The idea that they don't care at all about connecting stories is just not true. As is the statement that they never had a timeline till Hyrule Historia, which the devs have said referenced an internal document about the order of things.
label: Argument

text: It might not be set in stone but the concept of an overall timeline is certainly something they consider. The most central storytelling point of skyward sword is that it takes place at the beginning of the timeline, and every other plot point spirals out from this.

text: I know that 'Nintendo never let the timeline decide the games.' I'm not complaining about that. I'm complaining about the fact that BotW doesn't even line up properly or connect properly to Tears of the Kingdom. That both games are incompatible, and that it truly shows that Nintendo doesn't care about keeping their games consistent. Also using 'family games' isn't an excuse for inconsistency between titles. Other family series are able to keep consistent lore just fine. Stuff like Splatoon, or Pikmin (until Pikmin 4 came along and reset everything).
label: Argument

text: I mean, if you play the game it should be evident where and when it doesn't line up with Breath of the Wild or it's established lore. Breath of the Wild cleanly connected to Ocarina of Time. It made reference of it multiple times in multiple spaces- like the road to Zora's Domain, or the Great Plateau's Temple of Time initially being the exact same one from Ocarina of Time. Hell, they were setting things up for Calamity Ganon to be moreso related to Ocarina of Time Ganondorf (both via in-game references and in the MasterWorks/Creating a Champion lore book for the game). Then here comes Tears of the Kingdom, introducing a new founding of Hyrule, and thus making all of these allusions to Ocarina incompatible with the history they established. They changed who the 'man who became the Calamity' was, which also makes it to where none of the other Ganondorfs could exist after TotK's Ganondorf, despite it's Ancient past being the founding of the Kingdom of Hyrule itself (since the book says that there haven't been any male Gerudo made king after the man who became Calamity Ganon). On top of this, the game doesn't make sense within even it's own context (barring the Ocarina of Time stuff). Tons of NPCs forget that Link exists entirely, despite the fact that all the sidequests were canonically done by Link. He bought his house, and yet Bolson doesn't recognize him and acts like they've never met before. Tarrey Town exists, and yet none of the residents even recognize Link in the slightest. The only NPCs to know who Link are were the ones he interacted with in BotW's main story effectively, and even then it's pretty rough. Then you have the fact that everyone ignores that Shiekah tech ever existed, and the only people to mention that the Calamity ever happened was a school teacher and his class. They did this because they wanted people to be able to play Tears of the Kingdom without jumping into Breath of the Wild beforehand, but it feels like a situation where the developers wanted to have their cake and eat it too. I wouldn't be surprised if this was something that probably could have happened with other Zelda sequels if they took place in the same map- but if anything, this is why Zelda sequels shouldn't take place on the same map. If they aren't going to bother having continuity between their games- even when it's a direct sequel, then they shouldn't be reusing the same world as the previous game. As a whole though, Nintendo seems to be doing this across the board with their series. Pikmin did a massive lore reset with Pikmin 4 in order to be 'accessible,' despite the fact that 1, 2, and 3 were fine games to pick up without having to play previous entries.
label: Argument

text: I did play the game. I know many hold this opinion, but personally, I can't agree. There are even several direct references in the game. This even includes the Shekiah tech. text: Ocarina of Time is special because it's pure fairytale. Look at how Nintendo has gone back to realism. Fans forget because of the countable polygons, but early 3D games were meant to look real. Likewise, Zelda was meant to feel archetypal. However, just like 3D graphics drifted to something like painterly aesthetics, Zelda drifted to lore upon lore. Another thing fans may have forgotten is that Zelda timeline fascination begins with Ocarina of Time. So, my take is that Nintendo is trying to revitalize Zelda in this archetypal mould, both for a new generation of fans, and in preparation for the rebooting Holywood film. Ocarina of Time is perfect for this endeavour because it is steeped in fairytale conventions more than in lore of its own making. This reset is peddled by the 'generic' narration given in the remake's teaser. Link looks like a boy and less like a videogame mascot. So in short, I think Nintendo is revisiting Ocarina because it's the most fundamental Zelda. There's also a good chance that, for the same reason, the movie will be based most on OoT.
label: Argument

text: If anything the FFVII Remake is why this fear exists in the first place. text: I do see where you're coming from, but I don't really think that there's anything to be lost overall from remaking OoT. The only real remake that I ever feared displacing the original was Oblivion Remastered, because I was worried that the Oblivion modding community would leave the OG for it. Luckily, that didn't happen. With a game like OoT, where it isn't really centered around some modding community breathing new life into it after so long, the worst that can happen is a change in what first pop up when you look up guides or wikis for OoT. There is no massive modding community that risks being lost here, so OG OoT doesn't lose out on anything. The other argument that I think could be made is that the OoT remake will have been a waste of resources if it turns out bad, but then you have to ask wonder if a WW remake or a TP remake would have gone over any better. If they can't remake the quintessential game in the series in a satisfactory way, then no remake was ever going to turn out well. Oh, I guess another risk for the remake is that it drops some terrible retcon that seriously hurts the story. That'd definitely be an annoyance, but the fact that the Zelda games are so disconnected means that you could mostly ignore it aside from a few references here and there.

text: Sorry, but I should have been more clear. OoT has things like the randomizer, but Oblivion has things like new armor sets, new races, new dungeons, new questlines, and even, albeit very rarely, new worlds. To my understanding, though I don't follow the randomizer community at all, the randomizer focuses more on randomizing what's already there, rather than adding new content to the game. The OoT analogue, I think, would be ROM hacks more than the randomizer. OoT's ROM hacks largely fill the same purpose as the bigger quest mods, but I would assume that the process of modding the remake would be very different from making ROM hacks for the OG. It would be an alternative, rather than a flat upgrade, so there'd be a pretty big chance of the OG ROM hacking community surviving and staying with the original, rather than picking up and moving on to the remake. One undercurrent of thought that I ran into back in April of 2025 was that the Oblivion Nexus Mods community might end up moving on to the remaster, meaning that the original game would no longer get these massive content expansions by default. At best, you would have authors make mods for both communities, but with the primary focus being on the remaster. In the end, though, the remaster was unable to supplant the original for whatever reason.

notes: The 'Because' Test: Uses 'Creating a Champion' lore book as evidence to answer why.

text: Does the Master Works timeline confirm a True Founding? No, it confirms the Refounding theory by saying there have been no male gerudo leaders since TOTK Ganondorf in the founding era. Ardi is made leader of the Gerudo because Ganondorf betrays the Gerudo. The Gerudo also remember that Calamity Ganon was once a Gerudo and feel deep shame towards the royal family of Hyrule. But first of all, that doesn't make much sense, as there are several pieces of evidence that point towards the Zonai being Hylian's ancestors. Well firstly that's not presented in any sort of credible way and secondly, yes, there are Hylians after the zonai. This legend would be about these Hylians, it's a legend of this land. And if Hylia gave the Zonai the secret stones, it makes sense she did it while she was still alive in physical divine form (aka long before Skyward Sword). After SS, she only existed as a spirit... so did she somehow keep the Secret Stones in spirit form, for thousands of years, until the Zonai popped up on the surface? You're basically asking if she did her job, the one divinely mandated by the Old Gods onto her. Yes, she watched over the Secret Stones as she was commanded. She didn't hand them over until the Zonai, who themselves might be demigods. I don't see why it makes more sense that she did this while alive, she has no problem handing things out (Heart Containers and Stamina Vessels), directly interacting with the world (sealing the Horned Statue at some point and then moving the Horned Statue multiple times between BOTW and TOTK) and even directly speaking with people from her statues as she does multiple times in BOTW. The zonai worshipped her, no reason to think she couldn't give them the stones. It says that Hyrule prospers and that Calamity Ganon appears, and under there (in the midle of a double-wavey line mind you, meaning a long time passed), it says 'Ganon is revived and sealed away again many times'. The fact that it starts with Calamity Ganon means 'Ganon' thereafter refers to Calamity Ganon. It's referring to the calamity cycle. Impa mentions that there are countless legends prior to the one 10,000 years ago and that the people of that time 'thought it wise to utilize their technological prowess to prepare for Calamity Ganon's inevitable return', because it is a cyclical battle by that point. We're told that 'the history of the royal family of Hyrule is the history of Calamity Ganon'. The story is that CG has almost always been there since the sealing of Ganondorf. Obviously there's some gap time before the first instance and in between, but this cycle of CG vs the royal family is defining to this kingdom. The Deku Tree is said to have watched over the MS since time immemorial and it's wondered how many heroes he's seen come for the sword. The CaC timeline says Hyrule flourished, that Ganondorf appears and becomes Ganon, and that 'Ganon is revived and sealed away again many times'. CaC's timeline includes the Era of Myth and OOT. TOTK MW doesn't. In the CaC timeline, since it's including the era of myth and nothing is known about prior to 10,000 years ago, it's placed 'more than 10,000 years ago'. In TOTK MW it isn't including the era of myth, the '10,000 years ago' and 'more than 10,000 years ago' cover the founding era seen in the game and the sheikah golden age spoken of in BOTW.
label: Argument

text: 1. Aol shows us that there are multiple Gerudo factions. The OOT Gerudo are distinct different from Wild Gerudo. Maybe there hasn't been a Gerudo leader in the Wild faction, but one can still have existed in another one. Besides, how on earth would anyone know Calamity Ganon came from TOTK Ganondorf, when he never took the form of a pig, and the knowledge of his seal itself was so hidden that even the Royal Family eventually lost that knowledge? Makes much more sense that CG is the one that actually terrorized history and that history didn't erase. 2.
